region_of_origin: Domesticated in Papua New Guinea (~8000 BCE); Cavendish variety commercially dominant globally; imported to Singapore primarily from the Philippines, Ecuador, and Malaysia
food_subgroup: Ripe Cavendish banana, yellow peel with some brown spots (standard Singapore eating stage), medium size, peeled flesh only

serving.natural_language_portion: One medium ripe Cavendish banana with peel removed weighs approximately 120 g of edible flesh, the standard single-serve portion sold and consumed in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Banana, ripe and slightly ripe, raw — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 173944); cross-referenced with HPB Singapore Foodo database
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service SR Legacy + Health Promotion Board Singapore (Foodo)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values taken directly from USDA SR Legacy FDC ID 173944 (Bananas, raw) and confirmed against HPB Singapore Foodo. All per-100 g values match within rounding tolerance.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Banana (Cavendish, ripe) — University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2008
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI ~51 for ripe Cavendish banana confirmed in the University of Sydney GI Database. The University of Sydney GI Database classifies GI 51 as low (threshold: low <55, medium 55–69, high ≥70). GL per medium banana (120 g) = (51 × 23.0 × 1.20) / 100 = 14.1. Note: GI varies with ripeness — green banana GI ~30, ripe yellow ~51, overripe/brown ~65+. The value of 51 applies to the standard ripe yellow stage with minimal brown spotting as consumed in Singapore.

hypertension.key_factor: Very high potassium content (358 mg/100 g); low sodium (1 mg/100 g); magnesium 27 mg/100 g
hypertension.guidance: One medium ripe banana provides approximately 430 mg potassium (~12% of the 3500 mg daily target), making it one of the most potassium-dense common foods; potassium directly counteracts sodium-mediated vasoconstriction and is strongly recommended in DASH-pattern diets for blood pressure management.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low GI (~51) and moderate glycemic load (GL 14.1 per medium banana); ripeness-dependent GI variation; magnesium supports insulin receptor signalling
type2_diabetes.guidance: One medium ripe banana per day is acceptable for most T2DM patients given its low GI (~51) and moderate GL (14.1); patients should choose bananas at the yellow-with-minimal-brown-spots stage (GI ~51) rather than overripe/brown bananas (GI ~65+), and those with diabetic nephropathy (CKD) must restrict high-potassium foods including banana due to impaired renal potassium excretion.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High potassium (358 mg/100 g), dietary fibre (2.6 g/100 g), negligible saturated fat (0.1 g/100 g), zero cholesterol, vitamin B6 (0.37 mg/100 g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Banana is cardioprotective: its high potassium content supports vascular tone and reduces stroke risk, dietary fibre contributes to LDL reduction, negligible saturated fat and zero cholesterol make it compatible with heart-healthy dietary patterns, and vitamin B6 supports homocysteine metabolism relevant to cardiovascular risk.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Vitamin B6 (0.37 mg/100 g) for cytokine metabolism; magnesium (27 mg/100 g) for anti-inflammatory enzyme pathways
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Banana is well-tolerated in rheumatoid arthritis; vitamin B6 supports cytokine metabolism and immune regulation, and magnesium supports anti-inflammatory enzyme pathways, with no known RA-aggravating components.

llm_training.natural_language_description: The Cavendish Banana (香蕉, Pisang, வாழைப்பழம், केला) is Singapore's most commonly consumed fruit and one of the most universally eaten foods in the world. Available year-round at every Singapore supermarket, hawker fruit stall, and convenience store, ripe Cavendish bananas are eaten as a quick breakfast, everyday snack, or post-workout food. The fruit is elongated and curved with soft, sweet, creamy-textured flesh; the peel is removed before eating. Nutritionally, banana is most notable for its extraordinary potassium content — 358 mg per 100 g, yielding approximately 430 mg per medium fruit (120 g), which represents roughly 12% of the recommended daily potassium intake in a single serving. This makes banana one of the best dietary sources of this blood-pressure-regulating mineral. The glycemic index of ripe Cavendish banana is approximately 51 (low GI per University of Sydney GI Database thresholds of low <55), with a glycemic load of 14.1 per medium fruit — acceptable for most Type 2 Diabetes patients at one fruit per day. A critical clinical teaching point is the RIPENESS EFFECT: as a banana ripens, its resistant starch progressively converts to simple sugars, raising the GI from approximately 30 (green/unripe) to 51 (ripe yellow with minimal brown spots) to 65+ (overripe/brown). T2DM patients should select bananas at the yellow-with-minimal-brown-spots stage. Patients with diabetic nephropathy (CKD) must avoid banana due to its high potassium content and impaired renal potassium excretion. Banana is also a good source of vitamin B6 (0.37 mg/100 g), magnesium (27 mg/100 g), dietary fibre (2.6 g/100 g), and folate (20 mcg/100 g). The Cavendish variety dominates global banana trade and is the standard banana imported into Singapore from the Philippines, Ecuador, and Malaysia.
common_user_queries: how many bananas per day diabetic

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from USDA SR Legacy FDC ID 173944 (Bananas, raw), cross-referenced with HPB Singapore Foodo. GI value of 51 confirmed in the University of Sydney GI Database for ripe Cavendish banana. All ontology codes verified. Full multilingual coverage provided for Singapore context.
notes: Cholesterol is naturally zero (banana is a plant food containing no dietary cholesterol per USDA FoodData Central FDC ID 173944). Vitamin B12 is naturally zero (banana is a plant food; plant foods contain no vitamin B12). Vitamin D is naturally zero (banana contains no vitamin D per USDA FoodData Central). Trans fat is confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central. Monounsaturated fat (0.03 g/100 g) and polyunsaturated fat (0.07 g/100 g) sourced from USDA SR Legacy FDC ID 173944. Water content (74.9 g/100 g) sourced from USDA SR Legacy FDC ID 173944. GI note: value of 51 applies to ripe yellow Cavendish banana with minimal brown spotting; GI rises to ~65+ for overripe/brown bananas. GI classification: per University of Sydney GI Database standard thresholds (low <55, medium 55–69, high ≥70), GI 51 is classified as LOW. The input record labelled gi_level as 'low' which is correct per University of Sydney thresholds; a previous iteration incorrectly reclassified this as 'medium' — corrected back to 'low' in this iteration. CKD caution: patients with diabetic nephropathy or chronic kidney disease should restrict banana intake due to high potassium content (358 mg/100 g).
